definitions.definition: to get something that is useful, that gives you an advantage, or that is in some way positive, especially over a period of time
examples: The mayor has gained a lot of support from the teacher's union.
What do you hope to gain from the course?
Alternative medicine has only just started to gain respectability in our society.
It was her performances in Aida that gained her an international reputation as a soprano.
After you've gained some experience teaching abroad, you can come home and get a job.
From the late 19th century, European powers began to gain control of parts of the Ottoman Empire.
She's certainly gained (in) confidence over the last couple of years.
The data exists all right - the difficulty is in gaining access to it.
The thieves gained entrance through an upstairs window that was left open.

definitions.definition: If a political party or an idea or belief gains ground, it becomes more popular or accepted
examples: The Republicans are gaining ground in the southern states.

definitions.definition: to increase in weight, speed, height, or amount
examples: I gained a lot of weight while I was on holiday.
The car gained speed going down the hill.
Good economic indicators caused the share index to gain (by) ten points.
The campaign has been gaining momentum over the past few weeks.

definitions.definition: If a clock or watch gains, it works too quickly and shows a time that is later than the real time
examples: My watch has gained (by) ten minutes over the last 24 hours.

definitions.definition: an occasion when you get something useful or positive
examples: Whatever the objections to this sort of treatment, the gains in terms of the number of lives saved are substantial.
The minister was sacked for abusing power for his personal gain.

definitions.definition: an increase in something such as size, weight, or amount
examples: Side effects of the drugs may include tiredness, headaches, or weight gain.
After deducting costs, we still made a net gain of £5,000.
Oil prices rose again today after yesterday's gains.

to obtain, buy, or earn something
examples: He went to the shop to get some milk.
I think she gets about £40,000 a year.
We stopped on the way to get some breakfast.
I managed to get all three suitcases for under $200.
How much did he get for his car? (= How much money did he sell it for?)
Where did you get your shoes from?

definitions.definition: to receive or be given something
examples: I got quite a surprise when I saw her with short hair.
When did you get the news about Sam?
I got a phone call from Phil last night.
What grade did he get for the exam?
I got the impression that they'd rather be alone.
What did you get for your birthday?
We don't get much snow (= it does not often snow) here.
I managed to get a glimpse of him (= see him for a moment) through the crowds.
If you get a moment (= have time available), could you help me fill in that form?
She gets such pleasure from her garden.
If you can get some time off work, we could finish the decorating.
I can never get her to myself (= be alone with her) because she's always surrounded by people.

definitions.definition: to go somewhere and bring back someone or something
examples: Let me go get my glasses.
Can I get you a drink?

definitions.definition: to take someone or something into your possession by force
examples: Have the police got the man who did it yet?
Your cat got a bird this morning!

definitions.definition: to reach or arrive at a particular place
examples: We hadn't even got as far as London when the car broke down.
What time does he normally get home (from work)?
If you get to the restaurant before us, just wait at the bar.

definitions.definition: to reach a particular stage, condition, or time
examples: You earn loads of money if you get to the top in that profession.
It got to Thursday and she still hadn't heard any news.
I'm getting to the stage now where I just want to give up.

definitions.definition: to make progress or to improve
examples: She's taking flute lessons, but she really doesn't seem to be getting anywhere with it.
It's been hard settling in, but I feel like I'm getting somewhere at last.

definitions.definition: to become ill with a disease, virus, etc.
examples: I got food poisoning at that cheap little seafood restaurant.
Kids get all kinds of bugs at school.

definitions.definition: to become or start to be
examples: He gets really upset if you mention his baldness.
Is your cold getting any better?
Your coffee's getting cold.
After a while you get used to all the noise.
You're getting to be such a big boy, aren't you!
How did you get to be a belly dancer?

definitions.definition: to start to go or move
examples: We'd better get moving or we'll be late.

definitions.definition: to cause something to happen, or cause someone or something to do something
examples: She had to get the kids ready for school.
I'm trying to get this article finished by Thursday.
We get our groceries delivered.
Were you able to get the copy machine working?
I can't get my computer to work!

definitions.definition: to persuade someone to do something
examples: Why don't you get Nicole to come to the party?

definitions.definition: to do something to something or someone without intending to or by accident
examples: He got his bag caught in the train doors as they were closing.
I always get the two youngest sisters' names confused.

definitions.definition: sometimes used instead of "be" to form the passive
examples: I got shouted at by some idiot for walking past his house.
They're getting married later this year.
This window got broken.

definitions.definition: to move to a different place or into a different position
examples: I hit my head as I was getting into the car.
Get out of here now or I'll call the police.
The bed is too wide - we'll never get it through the door.
Getting up the ladder was easy enough - it was coming down that was the problem.
He got down on his knees and asked me to marry him!

definitions.definition: to travel somewhere in a train, bus, or other vehicle
examples: Shall we get a taxi to the station?

definitions.definition: to deal with or answer a ringing phone, knock on the door, etc.
examples: Hey, Ty, someone's at the door - would you get it, please?

definitions.definition: to have the chance to do something
examples: I never get to see her now that she works somewhere else.

definitions.definition: to understand or hear something
examples: I didn't get what he said because the music was so loud.
I told that joke to Sophia, but she didn't get it.

definitions.definition: to prepare a meal
examples: I'll put the kids to bed while you're getting dinner ready.

definitions.definition: to pay for something
examples: Put your money away - I'll get these drinks.

definitions.definition: to confuse someone and make them completely unable to understand or explain
examples: Give him a technical question - that'll really get him!

definitions.definition: something that you say when you do not know the answer to a question
examples: "How many ounces in a kilo?" "You've got me there."

definitions.definition: to annoy someone
examples: It really gets to me the way we're expected to actually laugh at his pathetic jokes!

definitions.definition: to make someone feel strongly emotional and often cry
examples: That part in the film when he finds out that his daughter is alive - that always gets me!

definitions.definition: to hit someone, especially with a bullet or something thrown
examples: The bullet got her in the leg.

definitions.definition: an occasion when someone leaves a place quickly, usually after committing a crime
examples: The two masked men made their getaway in a stolen van.
a getaway car

definitions.definition: to prove that something is true

definitions.definition: to start doing or using something
examples: I'll just connect the printer to the computer and then we'll be ready to go.

definitions.definition: to use your opportunity to play in a game
examples: It's your turn to go now.

definitions.definition: (of a number) to fit into another number especially resulting in a whole number
examples: 5 into 11 won't go.
3 goes into 15 fives times.

definitions.definition: to say, especially when a story is being told
examples: "I never want to see you ever again," he goes, and storms out the house.

definitions.definition: to become weak or damaged, especially from being used (too much), or to stop working
examples: After a gruelling six months singing on a world tour, it is hardly surprising that her voice is starting to go.
I really must get a new jacket - this one's starting to go at the elbows.
Her hearing is going, but otherwise she's remarkably fit for a 95-year-old.

definitions.definition: to produce a noise
examples: I think I heard the doorbell go (= ring) just now.
I wish my computer would stop going "beep" whenever I do something wrong.

definitions.definition: to be expressed, sung, or played
examples: I can never remember how that song goes.
"Doesn't it go something like this?" said Joan, and played the first couple of bars on her guitar.
The story goes (= people say) (that) he was fireds after he was caught stealing company property.
A headless ghost walks the castle at night - or so the story goes (= so people say).

definitions.definition: to happen or be found regularly or typically with each other or another
examples: Wisdom and maturity don't necessarily go together.
She knows all about the health problems that go with smoking.
Great wealth often goes hand in hand with meanness.

definitions.definition: to be put in a particular place, especially as the usual place
examples: The sofa went against that wall before we had the radiator put in.
I'll put it all away if you tell me where everything goes.

definitions.definition: to be sold or be available
examples: The shop is having a closing-down sale - everything must go.
The painting will go to the highest bidder.
I bought some flowers that were going cheap.
"Going... going... gone! (= Sold!)" said the auctioneer, banging down the hammer.

definitions.definition: to look or be acceptable or suitable
examples: That picture would go well on the wall in the living room.
The TV would go nicely in that corner, wouldn't it?
If I wear the orange hat with the blue dress, do you think it will go?
Just remember that I'm the boss and what I say goes (= you have to accept what I say).
My parents don't worry too much about what I am up to, and most of the time anything goes (= I can do what I want).

definitions.definition: to be known (by a particular name)
examples: He had a scruffy old teddy bear that went by the name of Augustus.
In Britain, this flour usually goes under the name of maize meal.

definitions.definition: to develop or happen
examples: "How did the interview go?" "It went very well, thanks."
Things have gone badly for him since his business collapsed.

definitions.definition: an attempt to do something
examples: I've never done this before but I'll give it a go.
"This jar is impossible to open." "Here, let me have a go."
I want to have a go at finishing my essay tonight.
We can't do the work all in one go (= all at the same time).

definitions.definition: to try to make something succeed, usually by working hard
examples: She's really making a go of her new antique shop.
I can't see him ever making a go of accountancy.

definitions.definition: an opportunity to play in a game, or to do or use something
examples: Hey, it's Ken's go now! You've just had your go.
Please can I have a go (= can I ride) on your bike?
I'll have a go at driving for a while if you're tired.

definitions.definition: to criticize someone
examples: My Dad's always having a go at me about getting a proper job.

definitions.definition: the condition of being energetic and active
examples: You're full of go this morning.
He doesn't have much go about him, does he?

definitions.definition: a Chinese game played on a square board in which two players try to surround each others' stones (= pieces)
examples: Around 50m people in east Asia play go.
Go was all the rage in Japanese courtly circles by the 11th century.

definitions.definition: to make a person or an animal react or do something by continuously annoying or upsetting them
examples: She seemed determined to goad him into a fight.
He refused to be goaded by their insults.
The runner was goaded on by his desire to keep up with the others.
A group of children were goading (= laughing at or pushing) another child in the school playground.

definitions.definition: to touch an animal with the end of a pointed object in order to make it angry or encourage it to do something
examples: The picador goaded the bull with a spear.
Dogs were made furious by being goaded with sticks.

definitions.definition: something that encourages someone to do something, especially by annoying or upsetting them
examples: His speech should serve as a goad to action.

definitions.definition: a stick for poking an animal to make it do what you want
examples: A boy walked alongside the ox-train, prodding the oxen with a metal pointed goad.

definitions.definition: past simple and past participle of guess
to give an answer to a particular question when you do not have all the facts and so cannot be certain if you are correct
examples: I didn't know the answer, so I had to guess.
On the last question, she guessed right/wrong.
Guess when this was built.
I guessed (that) she was your sister.
She asked me to guess her age.
I guessed the total amount to be about £50,000.

definitions.definition: to give the correct answer or make the correct judgment
examples: I bet you can't guess how old he is.
She guessed the answer on her first try.
"You have a new job, don't you?" "Yes, how did you guess?"

definitions.definition: used before telling someone something interesting or surprising
examples: Guess what? We won the game 4–0.

definitions.definition: used when you believe something is true or likely but are not certain
examples: I guess (that) things are pretty hard for you now.

definitions.definition: someone's opinion about something that is formed without any knowledge of the situation
examples: "I wonder why she's not here." "My guess is that her car has broken down."

definitions.definition: If a piece of information is anyone's guess, no one knows it
examples: "So what's going to happen now?" "That's anyone's guess."

definitions.definition: a person who is staying with you, or a person you have invited to a social occasion, such as a party or a meal
examples: 150 guests were invited to the wedding.

examples: We would like to remind all our guests to leave their keys at reception before they depart.
